title: Battle of Normandy (Operation Overlord)
summary: The Allied invasion of German-occupied France at Normandy, codenamed Operation Overlord, was the largest seaborne invasion in history. The German defense under Erwin Rommel and Gerd von Rundstedt attempted to repel the invasion at the beaches, but the Allies secured a beachhead and broke out in Operation Cobra.
background: Since 1942, both Stalin and the Western Allies pressed for a second front in Western Europe. After campaigns in North Africa and Italy, Operation Overlord was planned for spring 1944. Germany's Atlantic Wall fortifications defended the French coastline, but Rommel and Rundstedt disagreed on how to use the Panzer reserves.

axis.strength: 380,000 troops in Normandy, 850,000 total in France

allied.strength: 156,000 on D-Day; 2,052,299 total by campaign's end

phases.name: D-Day Landings
phases.description: Five beaches assaulted simultaneously. Airborne landings behind German lines preceded the seaborne assault. Despite heavy casualties at Omaha Beach, all five beaches were secured.

phases.name: Battle of the Bocage
phases.description: Allied forces struggled through the Norman hedgerow country (bocage) against fierce German resistance. Caen, a key objective, was not captured until July 9.

phases.name: Operation Cobra
phases.description: American breakout from Saint-Lô following carpet bombing. Patton's 3rd Army exploited the gap, beginning the breakout from Normandy.

phases.name: Falaise Pocket
phases.description: German forces were encircled at Falaise. Though many escaped the closing 'gap', the German Army in Normandy was shattered as a fighting force.

outcome: Allied victory. The liberation of Paris on 25 August 1944 effectively ended the Normandy campaign. The German Army in the West suffered catastrophic losses and retreated to the German border.
significance: The decisive turning point on the Western Front. The successful Allied landing opened a second major front in Western Europe, forcing Germany to fight on two fronts simultaneously and beginning the liberation of occupied Western Europe.
